mappings.rationaleAI: The Etruscan civilisation with its city-states, art, religious practices, and influence on Roman culture directly addresses Etruscan origins, cities, and their formative influence (A.3.a.i).

The Roman Republic covers its political institutions, military expansion, Punic Wars, and civil wars — comprehensively mapping to the early Republic (D.3), middle republic (D.4), and late Republic (D.5).
Alexander the Great's conquest of the Persian Empire and the Hellenistic age directly addresses the empire of Alexander (B.2.h).
Julius Caesar's conquest of Gaul, crossing the Rubicon, civil war with Pompey, and assassination directly address Caesar's campaigns and the alliance/conflict period (D.5.e).
The Gracchi brothers' agrarian reforms and political violence directly address the reform movement of the Gracchi (D.5.a).
The Augustan Age covers Augustus's establishment of the principate, moral reforms, and literary achievements of Virgil, Horace, and Ovid — directly relevant to E.1.a and E.1.d.
Tiberius as the second emperor covers the consolidation of the principate, Sejanus, and the transition from Republic to Empire — directly relevant to the empire under Tiberius (E.1.e).
Nero's reign covers the Great Fire, persecution of Christians, and political tyranny — directly relevant to the empire under Nero (E.1.e).
Constantine's conversion, Edict of Milan, and founding of Constantinople directly address Constantine the Great's reign (E.4.e.ii).
Julian the Apostate's attempt to restore paganism directly addresses Julian's reign and the attempt to restore the old empire (E.4.f.ii).

mappings.episodeTitle: The Roman Empire's Collapse in the 5th century
mappings.rationaleAI: The collapse of the Western Roman Empire directly addresses the eclipse of the empire in the West and Germanic invasions (E.4.g).
Pericles covers Athenian democracy, the Parthenon, the Delian League, and the Funeral Oration — directly relevant to Athens in the age of Pericles (B.2.a).
The Battle of Salamis as the decisive naval engagement against Xerxes directly addresses the Greco-Persian Wars (B.1.c.viii).
Thermopylae as part of the Persian invasion directly addresses the Greco-Persian Wars (B.1.c.viii).
Sparta's constitution, agoge, helots, and rivalry with Athens directly addresses the emergence of Spartan dominance (B.1.c.ii).
Solon's reforms including debt cancellation and political restructuring directly address the reforms of Solon at Athens (B.1.c.ii).
Thebes's hegemony under Epaminondas after Leuctra directly addresses the era of the Theban hegemony (B.2.d).
Carthage's destruction covers the Punic Wars — directly relevant to the First and Second Punic Wars (D.4.a) and the Third Punic War (D.4.b.ii).
Virgil's Aeneid as Augustan literature directly addresses the culture of the Augustan Age (E.1.d).
Roman Britain from the Claudian invasion to the withdrawal directly addresses the annexation of Britain under Claudius (E.1.e).
The oath in Greek and Roman society including Athenian democracy and Roman law addresses social and political organization (B.1.b) and Roman institutional development (D.2.c).
The Druids as priestly elite of Celtic societies directly addresses the Celts and their religion and social organization (A.3.b.i).
The Picts as peoples of northern Scotland with resistance to Roman expansion address non-Greek peoples of the Balkan Peninsula and non-Classical European peoples (A.3.a.iii).
The Sassanid Empire's rivalry with Rome is relevant to civil wars and conflict with Parthia under the Severan dynasty (E.4.a).
The Iron Age and its social and political transformations across Eurasia including Celtic Europe directly address non-Classical Europe in the Iron Age (A.3).
